questions.question: Prove that any algorithm that finds the maximum of n elements requires at least n-1 comparisons in the worst case.
keyPoints: Consider the adversary argument - each comparison provides at most 1 bit of information
The maximum must be compared with every other element to eliminate it as a candidate
If element x is not compared with all others, it could potentially be the maximum
Therefore, n-1 distinct elements must lose to the maximum in comparisons
This is a tight bound - the simple algorithm achieves it

questions.question: Using the loop invariant method, prove the correctness of insertion sort.
keyPoints: Define invariant: elements 0 to i-1 are sorted and contain the original i smallest elements
Initialization: i=1, first element alone is trivially sorted
Maintenance: inserting element at position i into sorted portion 0 to i-1 maintains the invariant
Termination: when i=n, the array 0 to n-1 is sorted and contains all n elements
The algorithm maintains the invariant through each iteration

questions.question: Differentiate between best case, worst case, and average case analysis with an example.
keyPoints: Best case: input for which algorithm performs minimum work
Worst case: input causing maximum work (upper bound guarantee)
Average case: expected performance over random inputs (requires probabilistic assumption)
Example: QuickSort - best/average O(n log n), worst O(n²)
Average case often more practical but harder to analyze precisely

questions.question: Explain the Master Theorem and identify its three cases with conditions.
keyPoints: Master Theorem solves recurrences of form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)
Compare f(n) with n^(log_b a)
Case 1: f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - ε)) → Θ(n^(log_b a))
Case 2: f(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a) * log^k n) → Θ(n^(log_b a) * log^(k+1) n)
Case 3: f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b a + ε)) and regularity condition → Θ(f(n))

questions.question: What is the difference between adaptive and non-adaptive algorithms? Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Adaptive algorithms adjust behavior based on input characteristics
Non-adaptive algorithms execute same operations regardless of input
Insertion Sort is adaptive: O(n) on sorted input, O(n²) on reverse sorted
Binary Search is non-adaptive: always O(log n) regardless of input
Adaptive algorithms often exploit structure in real-world data

questions.question: Analyze the time complexity of the following algorithm: for i = 1 to n: for j = 1 to i: for k = 1 to j: print(i,j,k)
keyPoints: The innermost statement executes once per triple (i,j,k) satisfying 1 ≤ k ≤ j ≤ i ≤ n
Counting: sum over i=1 to n of sum over j=1 to i of j
Outer sum: sum of triangular numbers = n(n+1)(n+2)/6
Time complexity = Θ(n³)

questions.question: Define optimal substructure and greedy-choice property. Explain why the Fractional Knapsack problem can be solved by a greedy approach while the 0/1 Knapsack problem cannot, providing specific examples.
keyPoints: Optimal substructure: optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems
Greedy-choice property: local optimal choice leads to global optimal solution
Fractional Knapsack: can take fractions of items, greedy by value/weight ratio works
0/1 Knapsack: must take whole items, greedy fails on counterexample

Counterexample: capacity=50, items (60,10), (100,20), (120,30). Greedy picks items 2,3 (value 220, weight 50). Optimal is items 1,2 (value 160, weight 20) - wait, better: items 2,3 is better. Let me recalculate: Actually greedy picks items with best ratio first: item1 (6), item2 (5), item3 (4). Picks item1 (10), remaining capacity 40, can't pick item2 (20 weight) - picks item3 weight 30. Total: value=60+120=180, weight=40. Alternative: item2+item3=100+120=220, weight=50. Actually greedy by ratio gives 220. Consider (60,10), (100,20), (120,30) with capacity=50: Greedy by ratio: item1(6), item2(5), item3(4). Takes item1 (10), capacity 40. Then item2 (20), capacity 20. Then item3 (30) won't fit. Total: 60+100=160, weight=30. Optimal: item2+item3=220, weight=50. This proves greedy fails.

questions.question: Solve the recurrence T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n² using the Master Theorem, and verify your answer by the substitution method. Also compute the exact solution for T(1) = 1.
keyPoints: Using Master Theorem: a=4, b=2, n^(log₂4)=n². Since f(n)=n² = Θ(n²), this is Case 2
By Master Theorem: T(n) = Θ(n² log n)
Substitution method: assume T(n) ≤ c n² log n for some c
Verify: T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n² ≤ 4(c(n/2)² log(n/2)) + n² = 4c(n²/4)(log n - 1) + n² = cn²(log n - 1) + n² = cn² log n - cn² + n²
For c ≥ 1, this is ≤ cn² log n, proving T(n) = O(n² log n)
For exact solution: T(1)=1, T(2)=4(1)+4=8, T(4)=4(8)+16=48, T(8)=4(48)+64=256, pattern suggests T(n) = n² log₂ n + n²

questions.question: Prove that any comparison-based sorting algorithm requires Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case.
keyPoints: Consider decision tree model - each internal node is a comparison, leaves are permutations
There are n! possible permutations (output orders) the algorithm must distinguish
A binary tree of height h has at most 2^h leaves
We need 2^h ≥ n! to distinguish all permutations
So h ≥ log₂(n!) = Ω(n log n) by Stirling's approximation
This is a fundamental lower bound for comparison-based sorting
